narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and family background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 (above the average of 3.21), LSAT score of 32 (below the typical 36.70), Year 1 grade decile of 6 (slightly above average), Year 3 decile of 8 (well above average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 70% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, your Year 3 grade decile, and your family income bracket, ranked by their impact on pushing the prediction one way or the other. Above all, your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as it stood notably lower than what the model sees in students who typically pass. In contrast, your strong Year 3 decile ranking worked significantly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success by showing late academic improvement beyond most peers. Your middle family income also contributed toward the failure side, though less forcefully, as the model associates it with slightly higher risks compared to higher brackets in similar profiles. Overall, while your undergraduate GPA and full-time status were positive, they couldn't offset the dominant drag from the LSAT in the model's eyes. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows solid later grades but lags in the key entry benchmark of LSAT, which often separates passers from those who struggle. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, as even a modest improvement could flip the model's prediction dramatically. Strengthening any perceived gaps in preparation through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level coursework might also help on a reapplication. You're clearly capable, especially with that Year 3 turnaround—keep building on it, and many schools will see your potential.
